conj.statement: 1. A policy framework conjecture 2. Confidence rating (0-1) 3. Top 5 states with most divergent needs 4. Urban/suburban/rural county distinctions 5. Three strongest cross-tier consensus points 6. Three geographic tier tensions to resolve1. All tiers recognize infrastructure as contested historical text 2. All require decolonial auditing of archival practices 3. All need participatory methods that avoid extractive research1. State standardization vs local specificity 2. Rural resource legacy vs climate adaptation needs 3. Urban data abundance vs rural data povertyAll tiers agree 19th-century infrastructure (railroads, canals, telegraphs) was not neutral but *designed to enforce power hierarchies* (e.g., railroad land grants to white settlers, canal construction using enslaved labor). 2. **Archival Violence Requires Active Repair**: Every tier identifies *deliberate gaps* in 19th-century records (e.g., erased Indigenous land claims, suppressed labor strike reports), demanding participatory "counter-archiving" as policy. 3. **Climate as Material-Historical Catalyst**: 2026 climate disasters (e.g., California wildfires, Louisiana coastal loss) are universally recognized as *material manifestations of 19th-century policy choices* (e.g., wetland drainage for agriculture, coal-dependent energy).
conj.state_variations: California (extreme climate vulnerability + tech capital); Texas (energy transition tensions + border dynamics); Louisiana (coastal erosion + colonial legacy); New York (finance capital + immigrant communities); Alaska (resource extraction + Indigenous sovereignty)
